sections.title: Regular -ER Verbs
sections.explanation: The largest group of French verbs ends in -er in the infinitive (parler, aimer, danser). To conjugate in the present tense, remove -er to get the stem, then add the personal endings: je -e, tu -es, il/elle -e, nous -ons, vous -ez, ils/elles -ent. Example: parler (to speak) → stem: parl- → je parle, tu parles, il/elle parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils/elles parlent. Important pronunciation note: the endings -e, -es, and -ent are all silent — je parle, tu parles, and ils parlent all sound identical at the end. The -ons ending sounds like 'ohn' and -ez sounds like 'ay'. Spelling changes: verbs ending in -ger (manger, nager) add a silent e before -ons to keep the soft g sound: nous mangeons. Verbs ending in -cer (commencer, lancer) change c to ç before -ons: nous commençons.
sections.tip: Three endings are silent in speech: -e, -es, and -ent. This means je parle, tu parles, and ils parlent all sound the same at the end. Only -ons ('ohn') and -ez ('ay') are clearly pronounced. For -ger verbs, always write nous mangeons — the extra e keeps the g soft.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'parler' (to speak) for 'je'?
exercises.explanation: The je form of -ER verbs drops -er and adds -e: parl + e = parle. The final -e is silent.

exercises.prompt: Tu ___ (écouter) de la musique. (You listen to music.)
exercises.hint: The tu ending for -ER verbs is -es (silent)
exercises.explanation: écouter → stem écout- + es = écoutes. The -es ending is silent, so tu écoutes and je écoute sound nearly the same.

exercises.prompt: How do you conjugate 'danser' (to dance) for 'nous'?
exercises.explanation: The nous ending for -ER verbs is -ons: dans + ons = dansons. This ending sounds like 'ohn'.

exercises.hint: The vous ending for -ER verbs is -ez, pronounced 'ay'
exercises.explanation: regarder → stem regard- + ez = regardez. The -ez ending sounds like 'ay', just like the word 'et' (and).

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct form of 'manger' (to eat) for 'nous'?
exercises.explanation: Verbs ending in -ger add a silent e before -ons to preserve the soft g sound: mang + e + ons = mangeons. Without the e, the g would be hard as in 'gare'.

exercises.prompt: How many of these forms sound different at the end: je parle / tu parles / ils parlent?
options: None — all three endings are silent
Two are silent, one is pronounced
exercises.explanation: The endings -e, -es, and -ent are all silent. Je parle, tu parles, and ils parlent are all pronounced 'parl'. This is a key feature of French pronunciation.

exercises.prompt: Elle ___ (travailler) à Lyon. (She works in Lyon.)
exercises.hint: The il/elle ending for -ER verbs is -e (silent)
exercises.explanation: travailler → stem travaill- + e = travaille. The final -e is silent. Note that the double-l in travailler is pronounced like a 'y' sound.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'aimer' (to like/love) for 'ils'?
exercises.explanation: The ils/elles ending for -ER verbs is -ent: aim + ent = aiment. The -ent is completely silent — ils aiment sounds identical to il aime.

sections.title: Regular -IR and -RE Verbs
sections.explanation: French has two other major verb groups: -IR verbs and -RE verbs. For regular -IR verbs (finir, choisir, réussir), remove -ir and add: je -is, tu -is, il/elle -it, nous -issons, vous -issez, ils/elles -issent. Notice the -iss- that appears in all plural forms — this is a key marker of this verb group. Example: finir (to finish) → je finis, tu finis, il finit, nous finissons, vous finissez, ils finissent. For -RE verbs (vendre, attendre, répondre), remove -re and add: je -s, tu -s, il/elle [nothing], nous -ons, vous -ez, ils/elles -ent. Example: vendre (to sell) → je vends, tu vends, il vend, nous vendons, vous vendez, ils vendent. The il/elle form of -RE verbs is just the bare stem with no ending added.
sections.tip: The -iss- infix is the signature of regular -IR verbs in the plural: finissons, finissez, finissent. For -RE verbs, the il/elle form has no ending at all — just the stem: il vend, il attend, il répond. Also note: the final -s and -d in je vends and il vend are both silent.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'finir' (to finish) for 'je'?
exercises.explanation: The je form of regular -IR verbs drops -ir and adds -is: fin + is = finis. Both je and tu take -is in the singular.

exercises.prompt: Nous ___ (choisir) un restaurant. (We choose a restaurant.)
exercises.hint: The nous form of -IR verbs adds -iss- before -ons
exercises.explanation: choisir → stem chois- + issons = choisissons. The -iss- infix appears in all plural forms of regular -IR verbs.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct nous form of 'réussir' (to succeed)?
exercises.explanation: Regular -IR verbs insert -iss- before the plural endings: réuss + issons = réussissons. This -iss- is the hallmark of this verb class.

exercises.prompt: Il ___ (vendre) sa voiture. (He sells his car.)
exercises.hint: The il/elle form of -RE verbs has no added ending — just the stem
exercises.explanation: vendre → stem vend- + nothing = vend. Unlike -ER and -IR verbs, the il/elle form of -RE verbs adds no ending at all. The final d is silent.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'attendre' (to wait) for 'tu'?
exercises.explanation: The tu form of -RE verbs adds -s to the stem: attend + s = attends. The final -s is silent.

exercises.prompt: How do 'finir' and 'vendre' differ in the ils/elles form?
exercises.explanation: Regular -IR verbs: ils finissent (with -iss-). Regular -RE verbs: ils vendent (stem + ent, no -iss-). The -iss- is exclusive to the -IR group.

exercises.prompt: Vous ___ (répondre) à l'email. (You reply to the email.)
exercises.hint: The vous ending for -RE verbs is -ez
exercises.explanation: répondre → stem répond- + ez = répondez. The vous ending -ez is the same for -ER, -IR, and -RE verbs, always pronounced 'ay'.
remplir is a regular -IR verb: ils remplissent (stem rempl- + issent). Double-s is required: remplissent, not remplisent.
